plumage.spots: White spots on head and breast
plumage.facial_disk: Rounded, pale-bordered, parabolic reflector for auditory localization

primary_call.name: Four-note territorial hoot
primary_call.temporal_consistency: Remarkably consistent within individuals (Forsman et al. 1984)
primary_call.peak_activity: First 3 hours after sunset, last hour before sunrise

theory_nodes.concept: Rhythmic motif (4-note cell as compositional building block)
examples: Four notes with distinctive rhythmic profile: long-short/short-long (choriamb)
Minimal pitch variation — identity through rhythm, not melody
Parallels Beethoven's Fifth: small number of notes, distinctive rhythm, immediately identifiable
The motif is the seed from which larger musical structures grow through repetition, variation, development
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The rhythmic motif — the smallest recognizable musical idea. Four notes with internal structure (downbeat, couplet, terminal sustain). The fundamental building block of composition.

examples: First nocturnal species in the circle — calls when diurnal species are silent
300-700 Hz band overlaps heron's 200-800 Hz — no interference because different time slots
Time-division multiplexing: same frequency channel, different temporal occupancy
The forest's acoustic environment is a frequency-time grid, not just a frequency spectrum
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Time-division multiplexing — the acoustic engineering principle. In a studio, two bass instruments share a frequency range by playing at different times (verse vs. chorus). The forest does the same: heron by day, owl by night.

examples: Presence = old-growth forest with full food webs; absence = degraded ecosystem
The owl's presence/absence reharmonizes the entire system — not one note but the key signature
Loss of the owl is not removal of one instrument but modulation to a diminished key
ESA listing (1990) recognized the owl as proxy for ecosystem health
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Key change (modulation) — the frame that determines what all other notes mean. The owl is not a note in the chord. It is the key signature. Its loss reharmonizes everything.

examples: 300-700 Hz optimized for propagation through dense old-growth canopy
Low frequencies attenuated less by foliage and moisture than high frequencies
Heron is low because it is large; owl is low because it needs to be heard across 3,000 acres in darkness
Overlaps degree 0 heron range but with entirely different communicative architecture
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Low-frequency broadcast — same register as degree 0, fundamentally different purpose. The heron's low frequency is incidental (body size). The owl's is intentional (propagation distance through canopy).

examples: Four-note pattern repeated with high temporal fidelity across calling bouts
Stated, silence (listening), stated, silence — call-and-response with the forest
Repetition is the mechanism by which a motif becomes a theme
A motif stated once is a fragment; repeated is a theme; persistently repeated is an ostinato
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Ostinato — a persistently repeated musical figure. The owl's territorial call is a nocturnal ostinato: the same four-note pattern, repeated across hours, asserting identity through iteration.

examples: Multi-layered canopy creates diffuse reverberation and low ambient noise
Owl's low-frequency hoot adapted to the acoustic properties of old-growth structure
Remove old growth → different reverberation, more wind noise, less efficient propagation
The forest is the resonating chamber — not background but part of the instrument
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The acoustic instrument — the forest shapes the owl's sound the way a concert hall shapes an orchestra. The architecture is part of the performance. Replace the hall and the sound changes.

theory_nodes.concept: Silence as attention (structured absence in rhythm)
examples: Long silences (30s to minutes) between hoot repetitions — the owl listens for responses
Silence between hoots is functional: the receiving end of territorial broadcast
Connects to degree 0: heron's silence was absence; owl's silence is attention
In music, rests between notes are as structurally important as the notes themselves
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Structured silence — the rests define the rhythm as surely as the notes. The owl's silence is not empty; it is the listening phase of a call-and-response dialogue. Degree 0's silence was foundation; degree 4's silence is function.

cross_references.title: Wings of the Pacific Northwest
cross_references.connection: Raptor/owl taxonomy and distribution; this release adds rhythmic motif analysis, temporal niche mapping, and Timber Wars history

cross_references.title: Living Systems of the PNW
cross_references.connection: Old-growth forest ecology, mycorrhizal networks, flying squirrel prey chain, indicator species concept

cross_references.title: Living Forest
cross_references.connection: Old-growth Douglas fir as acoustic instrument. Forest structure creates the reverberation environment the owl's call is adapted to.

cross_references.title: Weyerhaeuser
cross_references.connection: Timber Wars context. The owl's ESA listing changed Weyerhaeuser's business model and federal timber supply.

cross_references.title: Seattle 360 Music
cross_references.connection: Part A parallel: Floyd Standifer at degree 4 — both are territorial residents defending place through persistent, recognizable, repeated presence.

cross_references.title: Great Blue Heron (Degree 0)
cross_references.connection: Shared low-frequency register (200-800 Hz / 300-700 Hz). Temporal separation: heron diurnal, owl nocturnal. Same channel, different time slots.

cross_references.title: Pacific Wren (Degree 1)
cross_references.connection: Old-growth habitat overlap. Wren in understory by day, owl in canopy by night. Same forest, different floors, different shifts. FM cascade vs. rhythmic motif: maximum density vs. maximum spacing.

cross_references.title: Varied Thrush (Degree 2)
cross_references.connection: Both forest-interior species dependent on mature structure. Low-density vocalizations. Thrush crepuscular, owl nocturnal — temporal niche deepens.

cross_references.title: Black-capped Chickadee (Degree 3)
cross_references.connection: Direct predecessor. Chickadee's combinatorial syntax carries referential meaning. Owl's four-note motif carries territorial identity through rhythm. From language to composition.

part_a_parallel.shared_principle: Both are territorial residents defending place. Standifer held his position in Seattle's jazz scene for decades; the owl holds its territory in old growth. Both define their space through persistent, recognizable, repeated presence.
resonance_points: Territorial residency: Standifer stayed in Seattle and held his ground in the jazz scene for decades; the owl holds the same territory in old-growth forest year after year — both are defined by staying and defending place

Recognizable signature: Standifer's trumpet tone was immediately identifiable to anyone in Seattle's jazz community; the owl's four-note hoot is the most recognizable nocturnal vocalization in PNW old growth — both have a signature that announces who they are

Persistent presence as authority: Standifer's decades of presence gave him standing that newcomers could not match; the owl's long-term territory occupancy establishes priority over competing owls — seniority through persistence

Place-specific identity: Standifer was a Seattle musician in a way that could not be transplanted; the owl is an old-growth species whose existence is bound to the specific forest it inhabits — both are inseparable from their location

degree_arc.position: Rhythmic motif — the nocturnal temporal niche
degree_arc.progression: Silence (0°) → cascade complexity (1°) → sustained tone (2°) → referential syntax (3°) → rhythmic motif (4°)
degree_arc.key_insight: Degree 4 introduces the TIME dimension to the species engine. Degrees 0-3 partitioned acoustic space by frequency, density, and meaning. Degree 4 partitions by WHEN — the nocturnal niche that opens when the diurnal chorus falls silent. The four-note hoot is a rhythmic cell: the building block of composition, the smallest unit of musical identity. The circle has moved from sound (0-2) to language (3) to composition (4).

synthesis_types.degree_4: Rhythmic cell (motif — 4 notes with internal temporal structure)

register_progression.degree_4: Bass-baritone (300-700 Hz — returns to low register)

content_progression.degree_1: Identity + fitness (who is singing, how well)
content_progression.degree_3: Referential threat information (what is there, how dangerous)
content_progression.degree_4: Territorial identity through rhythm (I am here, this is my pattern, this is my time)

temporal_progression.degree_4: Nocturnal (first night species — time as territory)

ecology.primary_prey: Northern flying squirrel (Glaucomys sabrinus) — 50-80% of diet
ecology.prey_chain: Old-growth trees → mycorrhizal fungi → truffles → flying squirrels → Spotted Owl
ecology.nesting: Large cavities, broken-top snags, platform nests, witch's broom deformities (trees >200 years)
ecology.roosting: Thermal refugia under multi-layered old-growth canopy (Barrows 1981)

conservation.decline_rate: ~3.8% per year in Washington monitoring areas (Dugger et al. 2016)
primary_threats: Barred Owl competition and displacement (most acute current threat)
Habitat loss (70% of original habitat logged; continued state/private harvest)
Climate change (increased wildfire frequency in remaining old-growth)
conservation_actions: Northwest Forest Plan (1994) — 24.5 million acres of federal forest protected
conservation.historical_significance: Most politically significant bird in PNW history. ESA listing precipitated the Timber Wars (1989-1994) and the Northwest Forest Plan — the largest landscape-scale conservation plan in US history.

timeline: 1989: Federal court injunctions halt timber sales in owl habitat
1990-06-22: USFWS lists Northern Spotted Owl as Threatened (55 FR 26114)
1991: God Squad convened to consider ESA exemptions
1993: Clinton Forest Conference in Portland, Oregon
1994-04-13: Northwest Forest Plan Record of Decision signed

barred_owl_threat.origin: Eastern North America; expanded westward across boreal Canada
advantages: Larger body size (17-24 in vs. 16-19 in)
More generalist diet and habitat use
Higher fecundity (larger clutch, shorter generation time)
barred_owl_threat.management: USFWS authorized lethal removal in key recovery areas (2024 FEIS)

key_sites.name: Olympic National Forest
key_sites.description: Large tracts of old-growth and mature forest supporting Spotted Owl territories

key_sites.name: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
key_sites.description: Cascade Range old-growth with monitored Spotted Owl study areas

key_sites.name: Gifford Pinchot National Forest
key_sites.description: Southern Washington Cascades; mixed old-growth and late-successional forest

key_sites.name: Olympic National Park
key_sites.description: Protected old-growth rainforest; no timber harvest; long-term monitoring site

Franklin, A.B. et al. (1996). Studies in Avian Biology 17. [Survey methodology.]
